text: The surgeon utilizes a five-port laparoscopic approach, initiating dissection to the left of the duodenal jejunal flexure. The inferior mesenteric vein is elevated, and dissection proceeds posteriorly in the avascular plane.

The surgeon mobilizes the redundant sigmoid colon laterally, incising the lateral peritoneal fold and dissecting the sigmoid colon away from the ureter and gonadal vessels. The splenic flexure is not fully mobilized due to the planned abdominoperineal resection (APR).

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon anteriorly and incises the medial peritoneal fold just medial to the right ureter. A medial to lateral dissection is performed in the avascular embryonic plane, with careful preservation of the autonomic nerves visible during the procedure.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection, carefully preserving the ureter and gonadal vessels. Following adequate lymph node clearance, the inferior mesenteric artery is clipped and divided at its root.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection in the extra mesorectal avascular plane, using adequate traction to facilitate precise dissection up to the pelvic floor. The lateral peritoneal cut in the pelvis is extended, exposing the left iliac vessels.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery arising from the internal iliac artery. The left obliterated umbilical artery is ligated and divided. The internal iliac artery is dissected distally, with the middle rectal artery and inferior vesicle artery being identified, ligated, and divided.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal fold cut anteriorly to identify and clip the left vas. The right peritoneal cut is extended inferiorly to expose the right iliac vessels, and the superior vesicle artery, obliterated umbilical artery, middle rectal artery, inferior vesicle artery, and the vas on the right side are clipped and divided.

The surgeon joins the bilateral peritoneal cuts anteriorly and begins dissection in the prevesical space. The urachus is divided, and dissection proceeds inferiorly to separate the bladder from the anterior abdominal wall up to the pubic arch. Using a harmonic scalpel, the lateral bladder attachments are divided, exposing the levators and endopelvic fascia on both sides.

The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia on both sides of the dorsal venous complex, exposing the prostatic capsule. The dorsal venous complex is fully exposed, clipped, sealed, and divided using a harmonic energy device.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the periurethral tissue and transect the urethra. The right ureter is clipped and divided at the uretero-vesical junction, then released from its retroperitoneal attachments. Similarly, the left ureter is divided at the uretero-vesical junction and released from its retroperitoneal attachments.

The surgeon frees the left ureter as high as possible to facilitate uretero-conduit anastomosis. The mesocolon is divided at a desired level, and the distal descending colon is transected using a linear endoscopic stapler, marking the completion of the laparoscopic phase of the surgery.
